food_subgroup: Cooked fish dishes — sizzling hot plate preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Korean hot plate fish. FOODON_03301116 (fish food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Korean hot plate fish specifically. A00LC (Fish and fish products) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Korean fish dish preparation.

preparation_method: served on heated stone or iron plate
main_ingredients: white fish fillet (e.g. tilapia, basa, or similar)
Korean chili sauce or gochujang-based sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately one plate of hot plate fish (around 350 g), comprising a fish fillet with sauce and accompaniments as served at a Korean restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; adapted with Korean sauce and cooking oil additions estimated from USDA Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked fish (dry heat, mixed species, ~120 kcal/100g range). Values adjusted upward to account for Korean-style sauce (gochujang, soy sauce, sesame oil) and pan-frying oil, consistent with USDA Foundation Foods ingredient data. Composite estimate for a restaurant-style Korean hot plate fish dish. No direct HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry found for this specific dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — fish and mixed dishes with sauce; analogous estimate based on cooked fish with moderate-carbohydrate sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Korean hot plate fish. GI estimated at 35 based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked fish dishes with sauce (GI range 30–45 for protein-dominant fish dishes with moderate sauce carbohydrate). The sauce component (gochujang, soy sauce) contributes carbohydrate but the dominant protein and fat matrix suppresses overall GI. GL per serving calculated as: (35 × 6.8 g carb per 100g × 350g serving) / 100 = 8.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and gochujang-based sauce (~520 mg sodium per 100g; ~1820 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load from Korean fermented sauces; requesting reduced-sauce or sauce-on-the-side preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) with moderate carbohydrate content from sauce; high protein content supports glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot plate fish has a low estimated GI of approximately 35 and a moderate glycemic load per serving (~8 g), making it a generally suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without additional high-GI rice or starchy sides.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from fish offset partially by high sodium and moderate saturated fat from sauce and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fish component provides cardioprotective omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, but the high sodium content (~1820 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat from pan-frying oil present cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request lower-sodium sauce preparation and limit portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Plate Fish (石板鱼; Korean: 철판 생선, Cheolpan Saengseon) is a Korean-style sizzling fish dish in which a seasoned fish fillet — typically a white-fleshed fish such as tilapia, basa, or similar — is pan-fried and served on a preheated stone or cast-iron plate that keeps the food sizzling at the table. The dish is characteristic of Korean-Chinese fusion cuisine as served in Singapore, where it appears on menus at Korean bibimbap and rice bowl restaurants. The fish is coated in a savory-spicy sauce based on gochujang (Korean fermented chili paste), soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil, giving it a bold umami and mildly spicy flavor profile with a slightly caramelized exterior. The hot plate presentation creates a dramatic sizzling effect and keeps the fish warm throughout the meal. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-calorie, high-protein option (~148 kcal and ~14.5 g protein per 100g) with a low glycemic index (~35), making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management. However, the sodium content is high (~520 mg per 100g, approximately 1820 mg per serving) due to the fermented sauce components, which is a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids from the fish, as well as vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and vitamin D. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice or Korean side dishes (banchan). Common allergens include fish, soy, sesame, and gluten from soy sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in hot plate fish?
Is hot plate fish good for diabetics?
Is hot plate fish high in protein?
Can I eat hot plate fish if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA for Korean hot plate fish as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked fish data with sauce and oil additions estimated from USDA Foundation Foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable fish dishes with sauce. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked fish, dry heat) with additions for Korean-style sauce (gochujang, soy sauce, sesame oil) and pan-frying oil based on USDA Foundation Foods ingredient data. No direct entry for this dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as the dish uses non-hydrogenated cooking oils; confirmed negligible per USDA Foundation Foods data for sesame oil and standard cooking oils. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked fish dishes with moderate-carbohydrate sauce; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. GL per serving = (35 × 6.8 × 350) / 10000 = 8.3 g. 4. Serving size of 350g is an estimate for a single restaurant plate as served at Mincheng Bibimbap, Funan, Singapore. 5. Halal status is listed as false as this cannot be confirmed without restaurant certification; the restaurant may or may not be halal-certified. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked fish data and are approximate. 7. This dish is classified as Korean cuisine but is served in a Singapore Korean restaurant context; the Chinese name 石板鱼 reflects the Singaporean Chinese-language menu naming convention. 8. The food_id GD003908 is preserved from the field survey data seed record.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from seed field survey record. Derived nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy cooked fish adapted with sauce/oil additions. Added Chinese simplified 石板鱼 and traditional 石板魚 from input food_name. Populated all three health context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
